name: Ruy Lopez
description: The Spanish Opening. One of the most deeply analyzed openings, where White pressures Black's center by targeting the knight defending e5.

moves.commentary: The Ruy Lopez! Pins the knight that defends e5, creating indirect pressure on the center.
The Morphy Defense — asks the bishop 'where will you go?' Forces White to commit.
Maintains the pin. Retreating keeps tension and avoids giving up the bishop pair.
Counterattacks e4 while developing the kingside knight.
Castles, connecting rooks and preparing Re1 to support e4.
Develops and prepares to castle. A solid, classical approach.
Reinforces e4 and frees the f1 square for the bishop if needed.
Pushes the bishop away and gains space on the queenside.
The bishop retreats to a strong diagonal, still eyeing f7.
The Berlin Defense — counterattacks e4 instead of playing a6. Known as the 'Berlin Wall' for its solidity.
Castles, inviting Black to capture on e4.
Black takes the pawn. This leads to the famous Berlin endgame.
Opens the center to exploit Black's knight on e4.
The knight retreats, hitting the bishop on b5.
Captures the knight, damaging Black's pawn structure.
Recaptures, doubled pawns but gains the bishop pair.
Wins back the pawn, opening the position.
The knight leaps to an active post, eyeing g3 and d4.
The Berlin endgame begins. Despite no queens, the position remains complex and strategically rich.
Black loses castling rights but the king is actually safe in the center in this structure.
Gains space, drives the bishop back.
Prepares d4 to expand in the center.
The Marshall Attack! Black sacrifices a pawn for a fierce kingside attack. One of the most famous gambits in chess history.
White accepts. Declining with d3 avoids the storm but gives Black easy equality.
Recaptures with the knight, aiming to reroute it to f4 or e3 for the attack.
The Closed Ruy Lopez — Black keeps the center locked. A slower, strategic battle compared to the Marshall.
Prepares d4. White builds up slowly, aiming for a central break.
Prevents Bg4 pin. A key prophylactic move in the Closed Ruy Lopez.
The Breyer maneuver — the knight retreats to reroute to d7, supporting a future d5 break.
